rationale: Three Black Library Horus Heresy print omnibi from 2016 (Crusade's End, The Last Phoenix, The Razing of Prospero) followed by seven Primarchs Audio Dramas released between 2017 and 2019 (Stone and Iron, Malcador, A Lesson in Darkness, the Lords of Terra anthology, Illyrium, The Revelation of the Word, Morningstar). The audio shorts are all twenty- to thirty-minute character pieces, mostly two-handers between a primarch and one other speaker. HH-0237 The Lords of Terra is structurally an anthology re-issuing HH-0234/HH-0235/HH-0236 — flagged data_conflict (field: format, suggestion: anthology). Roster format on the three 2016 omnibi is correct as-given.

overrides.synopsis: Three novels and three short pieces collected as the opening movement of the Heresy. Horus Rising shows the Warmaster at the height of his power: Garviel Loken of the Sons of Horus rides at his primarch's side, the Great Crusade is bringing one world after another into compliance, and the future of the Imperium feels secure. By the close of False Gods that picture has darkened — Horus has been wounded on Davin and touched by something there, Erebus and Maloghurst are working a slow corruption, and Loken is no longer sure which of his brothers he can trust. Galaxy in Flames ends the arc with the dropsite atrocity at Isstvan III, where the legions Horus cannot turn are virus-bombed by their own. Three interleaved shorts — The Wolf of Ash and Fire, Death of a Silversmith, and Lord of the Red Sands — show Horus hunting beside Leman Russ in better days, the quiet murder that began the fall, and Lorgar Aurelian preparing the path ahead.

The duology and supporting shorts that frame the burning of Magnus the Red's homeworld, told from both sides of the fight. A Thousand Sons follows Ahzek Ahriman and the XV Legion through the Council of Nikaea and the patient, careful work the Crimson King does to spare his sons from the change that is already inside them — and shows the moment Magnus throws his psychic warning across the warp toward Terra. Prospero Burns turns the lens around: Kasper Hawser, a remembrancer adopted by the Space Wolves, walks among Leman Russ's warriors as the Rout is unleashed against the city of Tizca, and learns too late what the Wolf King thinks he is putting down. Custodes and Sisters of Silence accompany the assault, and the half-dozen shorter pieces around the novels deepen the grievance, the doctrine, and the cost of the burning.

The Emperor's Children at the moment of their corruption, told across two novels and the shorts that orbit them. Fulgrim opens with the III Legion at the height of its discipline, perfecting itself in war beside the Iron Hands, until a daemon-bound blade taken from the Laer drips its slow poison into the primarch's mind and ends with the duel that takes Ferrus Manus from the loyalist side. Angel Exterminatus carries the story past that betrayal: Fulgrim and Perturabo of the Iron Warriors lead a joint expedition into the Eye of Terror in pursuit of a weapon out of Eldar prehistory, and the two primarchs' very different ambitions begin to grind against each other. Around the novels the shorter pieces — among them The Crimson Fist and The Reflection Crack'd — follow Lucius the Eternal, Fabius Bile, Alexis Polux at Phall, and the slow unravelling of what the Phoenician once was.

Newly raised and still untested, the 33rd Grand Battalion of the Iron Warriors take the field for the first time with the primarch himself at their head. Perturabo means to use the campaign for more than a victory: the fight unfolds on a greenskin-held world held alongside the Imperial Fists, and the Lord of Iron is taking the measure of his fresh officers as much as he is taking the measure of his cousins under Dorn. As the orks press the lines and the VII Legion sets stone against the assault, Perturabo turns the action into an object lesson, drawing for his sons what he believes is the difference between Dorn's way and his own — a difference, he insists, that is not academic, and one that will define how the IV Legion fights for the rest of their long, grudging service.

flags.reason: the greenskin-held world where the 33rd Grand Battalion fights beside the Imperial Fists is not named in available source coverage

overrides.synopsis: With the Heresy at its height and the casualty rolls of a galaxy unspooling across his desk, Malcador the Sigillite leaves the palace and sits the long vigil at the bedside of Sibel Niasta, his personal astropath. She is dying, and they both know it. What unfolds across her last hours is not a war council and not a confession but something quieter — a conversation between two old friends about the life they shared, the man Malcador used to be, and the things the Sigillite has done in the Emperor's name that he is no longer certain he can carry alone. Glancing answers about the primarchs, about the future, and about the Emperor Himself drift up out of the talk, but they are not the point. The point is the friendship.

On a peaceful world at the far edge of the galaxy, an Imperial iterator sets down the official compliance of Piamen — a compliance the people themselves do not yet know is coming. Piamen freed itself from xenos overlords generations ago, and when the envoys of the new Imperium arrive its government politely refuses to take the Aquila. The VIII Legion answers, with Konrad Curze newly returned to his sons and eager to show them how he prefers to do this work. The story tracks two threads at once: the iterator's polished prose recording an orderly Imperial victory, and the events unfolding on the ground, where Curze and his captain Nivalus apply the terror tactics that will become the Night Lords' signature. For the legion, it is the first taste of what serving their primarch will mean.

Three previously released Primarchs audio dramas gathered as a single set. Perturabo: Stone and Iron sends the newly raised 33rd Grand Battalion of the Iron Warriors into the field beside the Imperial Fists, with the Lord of Iron taking the measure of his officers and teaching them the difference between Dorn's stone and his own iron. Konrad Curze: A Lesson in Darkness follows the Night Lords' first compliance under their newly returned primarch on the peaceful world of Piamen, where Curze applies the terror tactics that will define his legion. Malcador: First Lord of the Imperium turns away from the battle-lines entirely, sitting Malcador the Sigillite at the deathbed of his personal astropath Sibel Niasta for a long, elegiac conversation about friendship, duty, and the cost of the work the Emperor set him.

flags.reason: roster lists this release as a single audio drama, but The Lords of Terra is structurally an anthology re-issuing Stone and Iron, A Lesson in Darkness, and First Lord of the Imperium — three separately released solo-author audio dramas

overrides.synopsis: With the flagship Macragge's Honour under attack and the corridors burning around them, Ultramarines Sergeant Ammon finds himself pinned down beside an unlikely companion: Tarasha Euten, Roboute Guilliman's chamberlain, old in body but still sharp. Cut off from rescue and waiting for the firefight outside to settle, Euten passes the time the only way she knows — by telling Ammon the truth about a campaign the official histories of Macragge have already polished smooth. She walks him through the way young Roboute actually subdued the hill tribes of the Illyrium province, and the lesson she means him to take from it is not the one the chronicles record. By the time help arrives, the sergeant has heard a version of his primarch that the rest of the legion never will.

In the middle of the Shadow Crusade against Guilliman's Five Hundred Worlds, Lorgar Aurelian's meditations are broken by a Word Bearer son with strange news from the surface. An underground congregation has been found — Imperial citizens, hidden from the war, gathered around a battered copy of the Lectitio Divinitatus that they have been using to worship the Emperor as a god. Lorgar follows his chaplain down to the chapel and listens. The book in their hands is the same book he wrote in another life, the one he renounced when he swore himself to the Pantheon, and the irony of finding his abandoned scripture still doing its slow work in the heart of an Imperium he is now burning becomes the whole weight of the conversation. There is no battle here, only a primarch confronting what he chose to leave behind.

flags.reason: the world inside the Five Hundred Worlds where the underground Lectitio Divinitatus congregation is uncovered is not named in available source coverage

overrides.synopsis: Some time before the muster at Beta-Garmon and the final approach to Terra, Magnus the Red and Ahzek Ahriman walk together through the ruins of Tizca. The Crimson King has been pieced back into something close to whole, and Horus's plans for the siege require a weapon his sorcery alone cannot raise; Magnus believes the city of light still hides what they need, beneath the dust of what the Wolves left of it. The story is a long, intimate two-hander between father and son, picking through the broken pyramids and the older war that brought them low — and as Magnus once again plays the role of teacher, the conversation circles closer to the things he has never quite said to Ahriman, and to the shape of the legacy he means to leave in his most favoured pupil's hands.
